content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission,

P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
107 SOUTH BROADWAY • • • SUITE 2140A • • • LOS ANGELES, 90012


November 5, 1976

APC-11-059

Ms. Margie Gee
1511 North Maple Street
Burbank, California 91505


Dear Ms. Gee:

This letter confirms our telephone conversation
of November 4, 1976. In that conversation you described
the activities of the Burbank Anti-Noise Group (BANG)
and asked for advice regarding whether or not BANG had
any reporting requirements pursuant to the Political
Reform Act of 1974.

You indicated that BANG was created in 1969 and
that it was not formed primarily for political purposes.
Further, you indicated that BANG receives funds from a
variety of sources and these funds are not earmarked
for the making of campaign contributions or expendi-
tures. You indicated that BANG spent $141.68 in an
attempt to influence voters with respect to Proposition
'L' which was on the November 2, 1976 ballot in the
city of Burbank. Finally, you indicated that the $141.68
spent in connection with Proposition 'L' was the total
amount of funds spent during calendar year 1976 in an
effort to influence voters.

Based on the information you provided during our
conversation, BANG is not required to file campaign
disclosure reports. The Political Reform Act requires
campaign disclosure reports to be filed by committees
which receive or spend $500 or more for the purpose
of influencing or attempting to influence the action
of voters (California Government Code Section 82013).
Since the facts you related to me make it clear that
BANG does not meet the definition of a "committee"
which incurs reporting obligations, no reports are
required.


SACRAMENTO (916) 322-5660 • LOS ANGELES (213) 620-2305

Margie Gee
November 5, 1976
Page 2


I have enclosed copies of 2 Cal. Admin. Code
Section 18215 and 18225 which provide clarifications
of reporting obligations. Please contact me if you
have additional questions or if I may be of further
assistance.

Sincerely,

[illegible]

John C. Greenwood
Program Coordinator


JCG/lkh

Enclosures (2)

sections.question: You indicated that BANG was created in 1969 and that it was not formed primarily for political purposes. Further, you indicated that BANG receives funds from a variety of sources and these funds are not earmarked for the making of campaign contributions or expenditures. You indicated that BANG spent $141.68 in an attempt to influence voters with respect to Proposition 'L' which was on the November 2, 1976 ballot in the city of Burbank. Finally, you indicated that the $141.68 spent in connection with Proposition 'L' was the total amount of funds spent during calendar year 1976 in an effort to influence voters.
sections.conclusion: Based on the information you provided during our conversation, BANG is not required to file campaign disclosure reports. The Political Reform Act requires campaign disclosure reports to be filed by committees which receive or spend $500 or more for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of voters (California Government Code Section 82013). Since the facts you related to me make it clear that BANG does not meet the definition of a "committee" which incurs reporting obligations, no reports are required.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the Burbank Anti-Noise Group (BANG) have campaign disclosure reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act of 1974, given that it spent $141.68 to influence voters on Proposition L in 1976 and is not primarily formed for political purposes?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: BANG is not required to file campaign disclosure reports because it spent only $141.68, falling below the $500 threshold required by Government Code Section 82013 for committees to have reporting obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains clear question and conclusion sections. Telephone conversation referenced; letter confirms and provides written advice. Enclosures referenced (2 Cal. Admin. Code sections) but not included in provided text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You indicated that BANG was created in 1969 and that it was not formed primarily for political purposes. Further, you indicated that BANG receives funds from a variety of sources and these funds are not earmarked for the making of campaign contributions or expenditures. You indicated that BANG spent $141.68 in an attempt to influence voters with respect to Proposition 'L' which was on the November 2, 1976 ballot in the city of Burbank. Finally, you indicated that the $141.68 spent in connection with Proposition 'L' was the total amount of funds spent during calendar year 1976 in an effort to influence voters.

CONCLUSION: Based on the information you provided during our conversation, BANG is not required to file campaign disclosure reports. The Political Reform Act requires campaign disclosure reports to be filed by committees which receive or spend $500 or more for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of voters (California Government Code Section 82013). Since the facts you related to me make it clear that BANG does not meet the definition of a "committee" which incurs reporting obligations, no reports are required.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Margie Gee that the Burbank Anti-Noise Group does not have campaign disclosure reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act because its spending of $141.68 to influence voters on Proposition L falls below the $500 threshold.
